figures.caption: Figure 1. Integrated multi-omics analysis reveals systemic metabolic downregulation GCs 133 during early luteinization. (A) Workflow for integrative analysis of single-cell and spatial 134 transcriptomics data. (B) Identification of GCs in pre-ovulatory follicles at specified time points 135 post-hCG administration using integrated analysis. (C) PCA analysis of transcriptomic 136 differences in GCs from pre-ovulatory follicles. (D) Histogram of gene similarity scores 137 derived from model training data. (E) Scatter plot of tangram-predicted gene scores (x-axis) 138 versus spatial sparsity (y-axis) based on integrated transcriptome data. (F) Expression analysis 139 of randomly selected genes demonstrated enhanced spatial resolution and clearer localization 140 patterns in the integrated dataset compared to the original spatial transcriptomics data. (G) GO 141 enrichment analysis of the upregulated expression cluster. Left: Heatmap of cluster-specific 142 genes; Right: Enriched GO terms. (H) GO enrichment analysis of the downregulated expression 143 cluster. Left: Heatmap of cluster-specific genes; Right: Enriched GO terms. 144

Figure 3. Enhanced glucose uptake supports glycogen biosynthesis in GCs during early 241 luteinization. (A) Flow cytometric analysis of cellular glucose uptake capacity post-hCG 242 injection. Isolated GCs were incubated with fluorescent analog 2-NBDG (100 μM) for 30 243 minutes prior to analysis. Left: representative images; Right: quantification (n = 4 GC samples). 244 (B) Western blotting analysis of SLC2A1 and its upstream kinase P-PKCα in GCs post-hCG 245 injection (n = 3 GC samples). (C) Schematic of intracellular glucose carbon flux. (D) 13C-246 glucose tracing workflow. Pre-ovulatory follicles fasted 2 hours, then cultured in luteinization 247 medium with U-¹³C6-glucose (sole carbon source) for 6 hours before GC isolation and LC-248 MS/MS analysis. (E) Quantitative ¹³C enrichment in glycogen (n = 3 GC samples). The control 249 was defined as the group cultured in hCG-free medium. (F) Changes in 250 glycogenesis/glycogenolysis proteins during early luteinization (n = 3 GC samples). (G) PAS 251 staining of glycogen in GCs during early luteinization. Scale bar = 100 μm. (H) Colorimetric 252 quantification of glycogen content (n = 6 GC samples). (I) Changes in 253 glycogenesis/glycogenolysis proteins throughout luteinization. (J) PAS staining of glycogen 254 throughout luteinization. Scale bar = 500 μm (high-magnification), 250 μm (low-255 magnification). (K) ATP content changes throughout luteinization (n = 5-6 GC samples). (L) 256 Radioimmunoassay of progesterone in ovarian homogenates throughout luteinization (n = 4-7 257 ovarian samples). (M) Vascularization changes assessed by CD34⁺ endothelial cells (red) 258 during luteinization. Nuclei counterstained with DAPI (blue). Scale bar = 50 μm. Data represent 259 mean ± SD. Significance determined by one-way ANOVA with Tukey’s post hoc test (A, K, 260 L) or two-tailed unpaired Student’s t-test (E, H) (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P 261 < 0.0001). Results shown are from one of at least three independent replicates; all experiments 262 produced comparable data. 263

Figure 5. hCG (LH) induces glycogen storage via MAPK-RUNX1-Insulin signaling axis. 365 (A) KEGG pathway analysis of signaling activation in GCs post-hCG injection. (B) Western 366 blotting analysis of INSR/IGF1R activity in GCs post-hCG injection (n = 3 GC samples). (C) 367 Experimental design for panels (D-F). (D) Western blotting analysis of glycogenesis related 368 proteins in GCs after insulin pathway modulation. (E) Flow cytometric analysis of glucose 369

Figure 7. Timely oral glucose administration enhances GCES and luteal function in 453 humans. (A) GO enrichment analysis of the downregulated expression cluster in human GCs 454 post-hCG injection. Left: heatmap; Right: enriched GO terms. (B) Expression of glucose 455 transporter genes in GCs post-hCG injection. (C) Expression of insulin pathway genes in GCs 456 post-hCG injection. (D) Experimental design for panels E to G. Volunteers were randomly 457 selected from women undergoing ART treatment and responding normally to controlled 458 ovarian hyperstimulation. (E) Colorimetric qualification of glycogen storage in GCs post-459 glucose intake; n = 16 (Con), 18 (GLU) GC samples. (F) qRT-PCR analysis of luteal gene 460 expression post-glucose intake; n = 3 (Con), 4 (GLU) GC samples. (G) Progesterone levels in 461 follicular fluids measured by radioimmunoassay post-glucose intake; n = 34 (Con), 28 (GLU) 462 samples. (H) A diagram depicting GCES. Data represent mean ± SD. Significance determined 463 by two-tailed unpaired Student’s t-test (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). The experiments 464 (E, G) were repeated twice with consistent results. 465

Figure 2. Metabolic attenuation and bioenergetic repression in GCs characterize early 181 luteinization. (A) Workflow for metabolic flux analysis. Pre-ovulatory follicles were fasted for 182 2 hours, then cultured in luteinization medium with U-¹³C6-glucose as sole carbon source for 6 183 hours before GC isolation and targeted metabolomics. (B) Quantitative analysis of ¹³C-labeled 184 glycolytic and TCA cycle intermediates. Left: heatmap; Right: quantification (n = 3 GC 185 samples). The control was defined as the group cultured in hCG-free medium. (C) Changes in 186 mitochondrial ultrastructure and ER-mitochondria contact sites following hCG injection. Left: 187 representative images. Right: quantification. Yellow outlines: mitochondria; red arrow: contact 188 sites. Scale bar = 500 nm. (D) MMP measured by JC-1 staining (n = 4 GC samples). (E) 189 NAD⁺/NADH ratio determined by colorimetric assay (n = 4 GC samples). (F) ATP levels 190 measured by luciferase bioluminescence assay (n = 4 GC samples). (G) Ultrastructural changes 191 in ER and Golgi apparatus in GCs following hCG injection. Top: representative images. Down: 192 quantification. Red outlines: ER; yellow arrow: Golgi apparatus; yellow outline: transport 193 vesicles. Scale bar = 200 nm. (H) Heatmap showing changes in the content of GPI-anchor 194 biosynthesis pathway intermediates post-hCG (n = 5 GC samples). (I) Quantification of N-195 linked (Stained by Concanavalin A)/O-linked glycoprotein (Stained by Peanut Agglutinin) 196 abundance in GCs by lectin blotting. Top: lectin blot; Down: quantification (n = 3 GC samples). 197 (J) Workflow for SEAP reporter assay. (K) Quantification of SEAP secreted into the 198 supernatant using chemiluminescence assay (n = 5 supernatant samples). (L) Changes in M6P 199 pathway gene expression post-hCG injection (n = 3 GC samples). (M) Flow cytometry analysis 200 of GCs with high-intensity Lyso-tracker green fluorescence. Red line: fluorescence intensity 201 threshold; cells right of threshold line classified as high-intensity fluorescent (n = 4 GC 202 samples). (N) Detection of changes in lysosomal acid phosphatase activity using colorimetric 203

Figure 4. Mobilization of glycogen storage to fuel luteal steroidogenesis during the 302 avascular phase. (A) Experimental design for panels (B-E). (B) Colorimetric quantification of 303 glycogen content in GC post-glucose injection (n = 6 GC samples). (C) PAS staining of 304 glycogen storage post-glucose injection; scale bar = 100 μm. (D) Alterations in NAD⁺/NADH 305 ratio and ATP contents post-glucose injection (n = 4 GC samples). (E) Progesterone levels in 306 ovarian homogenates measured by radioimmunoassay post-glucose injection (n = 5 ovarian 307 samples). (F) Experimental design for Ugp2/Gys1 knockdown in cultured mouse follicles. 308 Luteinizing GCs isolated at H15 for functional assessment. (G) Changes in GC glycogen 309 storage post-Ugp2 and Gys1 knockdown. The commercial scrambled siRNA serving as si-310 Control (si-Con). Scale bar = 100 μm. (H) Alterations in NAD+/NADH in GCs post-knockdown; 311 n = 3 GC samples. (I) ATP content in GCs post-knockdown (n = 5-6 GC samples). (J) 312 Progesterone in culture medium post-knockdown (n = 6-8 samples). (K) Experimental design 313 for in vivo lentiviral-mediated Ugp2/Gys1 knockdown. Luteinizing GCs isolated from CLs at 314 H15 for functional assessment. (L) Western blotting confirmation of Ugp2/Gys1 knockdown in 315 ovaries (n = 3 ovarian samples). (M) PAS staining of glycogen storage post-knockdown. Scale 316 bar = 100 μm. (N) NAD⁺/NADH alterations in GCs post-knockdown (n = 5-6 GC samples). (O) 317 ATP content changes in GCs post-knockdown (n = 5-6 GC samples). (P) Progesterone level 318 alterations post-knockdown; n = 10 (si-Ugp2), 15-21 (si-Gys1) serum samples. (Q) 319 Experimental design for Ingliforib treatment in vivo. (R) PAS staining validation of Ingliforib-320

Figure 6. GCES augmentation enhanced luteal function and pregnancy outcomes in 413 mouse and ovine models. (A) Experimental design for panels B to E. (B) PAS staining of 414 glycogen. Scale bar = 100 μm. (C) Effects of glucose intake on serum and ovarian progesterone 415 levels at GD15; n (serum) = 15 (Con), 12 (GLU); n (ovarian homogenate) = 30 (Con), 24 (GLU). 416 (D) Effects of glucose intake on fetal number and regression rates at GD15 (n = 15 pregnant 417 mice). (E) Effects of glucose intake on fetal and placental weights at GD15; n = 4 (Con) and 5 418 (GLU) pregnant mice. (F) Embryo transfer experimental schematic. (G) Effects of glucose 419 intake on the farrowing rate of transferred embryos. (H) qRT-PCR analysis of glycogen 420 metabolism genes in ovine GCs pre-(L0) and 12 hours post-LH injection (L12); n = 8 (L0), 7 421 (L12) GC samples. (I) PAS staining of glycogen in ovine GCs post-LH injection. Scale bar = 422 500 μm. (J) Experimental design for panels I to L. (K) Effects of glucose intake on serum 423 progesterone levels (n = 30 serum samples). (L) Effects of glucose intake on sheep pregnancy 424 rate after breeding. Data represent mean ± SD. Significance determined by two-tailed unpaired 425
